core_intuitions: Decode is memory-bandwidth-bound: each generated token re-reads the full weight matrix, so tokens/sec is ultimately capped by HBM-bandwidth ÷ model-bytes-read. Every serving stack is fighting to approach this ceiling.

The through-line tension: the more a runtime commits to one hardware target, the more aggressive its optimizations (kernel selection, fusion, machine-code emission) — but the less portable it becomes. Peak-performance and portability sit at opposite ends of one axis.

WHY TensorRT-LLM is fast = AOT compilation. PyTorch runs a Python interpreter that dispatches many separate CUDA kernels per layer, bouncing intermediate tensors through HBM. TensorRT traces the whole forward pass, fuses ops into a few mega-kernels, and emits a hardware-specific binary engine — fewer launches, less HBM traffic, no runtime dispatch.

The serving topology differs by stack: Triton wraps a TRT-LLM engine as pre/post/engine sub-models with the Executor doing in-flight batching; TGI splits a low-latency Rust router from a Python model server over an IPC socket. Same job, different process boundaries.

The KV cache is the dominant serving-time memory budget: after weights, remaining HBM ÷ (per-token KV cost × context length) sets the number of concurrent sequences. INT8 KV cache halves per-token cost and roughly doubles that concurrency — often a bigger win than any single kernel tweak.

At the KV-capacity boundary the scheduler policy decides behavior: guaranteed_no_evict refuses/queues new requests to protect latency; max_utilization admits more by evicting-and-recomputing KV blocks.

new_figures.name: trtllm-eager-vs-compiled-engine
new_figures.concept: Side-by-side of one transformer layer's forward pass: PyTorch eager dispatch (many separate kernels, HBM round-trips, Python interpreter) vs TensorRT-LLM's AOT-compiled fused engine (few mega-kernels, on-chip data reuse, machine code).
new_figures.rationale: This is the chapter's central mechanistic 'aha' — WHY compilation buys throughput — and it is currently only stated in prose ('PyTorch runs an interpreter that dispatches individual CUDA kernels... TensorRT traces the full forward pass, applies layer fusion... emits a binary engine'). No existing figure shows the fusion / dispatch / HBM-traffic contrast; the spectrum figure asserts the tradeoff but never explains the cause. Making 'ten kernel launches bouncing through HBM' vs 'three fused kernels keeping data on-chip' visual is what makes the whole TensorRT-LLM story click, and it directly grounds the closing 'Portability vs Peak Performance' section's three bullet points (profile-guided kernel selection, op fusion, machine code vs runtime dispatch).
new_figures.brief: Two panels sharing a top title 'One transformer decoder layer — two execution models'. LEFT panel, header 'PyTorch (eager, runtime dispatch)': along the far-left edge draw a thin vertical rail labelled 'Python interpreter'; from it, small dashed 'dispatch' tick-arrows point rightward into a top-to-bottom stack of ~9-10 SEPARATE small kernel boxes in reading order — LayerNorm → QKV GEMM → Attention → Output GEMM → +residual → LayerNorm → FFN-up GEMM → GeLU → FFN-down GEMM → +residual. Between every consecutive pair of kernels draw a short round-trip pair of arrows to a slim 'HBM' column on the right (write-out then read-back), and put a tiny 'launch' overhead pip at the top-left corner of each kernel box. Annotate the panel foot: 'each op = its own kernel launch; intermediates round-trip to HBM; interpreter dispatches every step'. RIGHT panel, header 'TensorRT-LLM (AOT-compiled engine)': NO interpreter rail (add a small badge 'built once, ahead-of-time'); the SAME ops are grouped into ~3 large FUSED kernel boxes drawn in the accent treatment — e.g. box 1 'fused: LayerNorm + QKV + Attention + Output proj', box 2 'fused: residual + LayerNorm', box 3 'fused FFN: up + GeLU + down + residual'. Inside each fused box show the sub-ops as faint chevrons flowing without leaving the box, and label a small on-chip 'SRAM' pill to signal intermediates stay on-chip; only the panel's single input at top and single output at bottom touch the slim 'HBM' column. Panel foot: 'ops fused into a few kernels; data kept on-chip between them; machine code, no dispatch'. Use ONLY illustrative small integers for the kernel counts (e.g. '~10 launches' left, '~3 launches' right) — these are structural, not benchmarks; invent NO throughput/latency/speedup numbers. Bottom strip spanning both panels (muted italic): 'Fewer launches + less HBM traffic + no Python dispatch → closer to the memory-bandwidth ceiling. The cost: a hardware-specific engine that must be rebuilt per GPU SKU.' Theme-safe: fused/compiled boxes and the 'built once' badge use var(--accent...) with theme-aware surface fills; eager kernels use neutral --surface/--ink-soft; HBM column and round-trip arrows use --muted. Everything must fit inside the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order in each panel and no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: trtllm-kv-budget-and-scheduler
new_figures.concept: HBM capacity as a stacked budget — weights (fixed) + KV-cache region — showing how per-token KV cost × context length sets the number of concurrent sequences, what happens at the capacity boundary under two scheduler policies, and how INT8 KV cache roughly doubles capacity.
new_figures.rationale: The 'Practitioner's Framework' worked example and two Key Takeaways hinge on one quantitative picture: after weights, remaining HBM ÷ (KV/token × context) = concurrent sequences, and INT8 KV halving that cost doubles concurrency. This is the chapter's practitioner payload and its interview-corner answer (max_batch_size sizing, guaranteed_no_evict vs max_utilization), yet it lives only as prose + arithmetic. A single budget-bar figure makes the weights/KV split, the sequence-slot filling, the capacity wall, and the INT8 doubling tangible — and it ties the two scheduler policies (unique to this chapter) to the exact moment they matter. It complements rather than duplicates the anatomy/paged-attention chapters because the framing here is the serving-capacity budget and the admission-policy fork.
new_figures.placement: The Tradeoff Space: A Practitioner's Framework
new_figures.brief: Main element: one tall horizontal (or vertical) 'HBM capacity' bar labelled at the ends as the GPU's total memory. Partition it into two clearly labelled segments: a lower/left segment 'Model weights (fixed after load)' and the larger remainder 'KV-cache region'. Inside the KV-cache region, tile it into a small illustrative number of equal 'sequence slots' (e.g. show 6-8 filled slots plus a couple of empty ones) and annotate one slot with a callout: 'one sequence ≈ per-token KV cost × context length'. Draw a bold 'capacity boundary' line where the slots run out. From that boundary, fork to two labelled outcome chips: (A) 'guaranteed_no_evict → new request waits in queue (protects TTFT/latency)' and (B) 'max_utilization → evict + recompute KV blocks to admit more (higher utilization, recompute cost)'. BELOW the main bar, draw a second, slimmer companion bar of the same KV-cache width but titled 'INT8 KV cache': halve the per-slot width so it holds ~2× the number of sequence slots, with a short label 'per-token KV cost halved → ~2× concurrent sequences'. Keep all counts as illustrative small integers and use relative proportions / labels ('weights' vs 'KV') rather than any invented GB figures, per-token byte counts, or sequence-count benchmarks. Theme-safe: weights segment neutral (--surface-3 / --ink-soft), KV region and filled slots in accent-tinted fills, the capacity boundary line and the two policy chips distinct but theme-aware; the INT8 companion bar echoes the KV accent. Ensure the two policy chips and the INT8 bar all fit inside the viewBox with no label overlap and an obvious reading order: bar → boundary → policy fork → INT8 doubling.

summary: This is a landscape/comparison chapter and is already decently served by four solid figures: the peak-performance↔portability spectrum, the Triton serving pipeline, the TGI router/model-server split, and the stack-selection decision tree — all correct and legible in both themes (two get minor 'refine' notes for text overflowing their boxes; two are clean 'keep's). The one genuine mechanistic gap is the chapter's central 'aha': WHY AOT compilation makes TensorRT-LLM fast — currently only prose. I propose a priority-1 static side-by-side of eager kernel dispatch vs a fused compiled engine (fewer launches, on-chip reuse, no Python dispatch). A priority-2 static KV-budget/scheduler figure makes the practitioner framework's capacity math and the guaranteed_no_evict vs max_utilization fork visual, plus the INT8-KV doubling. No animation is warranted — both new figures read fully as static frames.
